translation.content: "Music was a way to speak Arabic… It's my way of somehow trying to communicate to myself and to be confident in myself that I am, in fact, Lebanese," Jared Beeler, AKA DJ Plead, told Crack Magazine in 2022. The Sydney artist is often described as an Australian producer threading Arabic rhythmic structures through techno and post-dubstep, but this misses the point. His work doesn't place tradition and innovation at odds; instead, traditional percussion is embedded within contemporary club music, creating something new while maintaining a connection to heritage. Beeler first surfaced in the late 2010s with sharp releases on DECISIONS and Nervous Horizon. In the years since, he's become one of the most consistent producers making leftfield dance music. While his Lebanese heritage is often cited as a key reference point, what stands out is the tactile clarity of his drum programming, which he often digitally renders using the Maqam Rast scale. It feels fitting, then, that Beeler's RA Mix opens with Bruce's "Just Getting On With It," taken from Livity Sound's ten-year anniversary compilation in 2021. The Bristol label's catalogue embodies the same tingling rhythmic experimentation that has defined much of Beeler's work. If his music draws directly from the vocabulary of Lebanese percussion, RA.1028 expands the frame, tracing parallel approaches across scenes and histories, from Iran (Flower Storm) to London (Kode 9 vs. Badawi) to Miami (Johnny From Space) and right back home; the 90-minute mix includes several of his own unreleased tracks. Whether it's the dry snap of darbuka-like hits or sub-bass frequencies that land with chest-caving impact, RA.1028 is a kaleidoscopic tour de force, and a welcome reminder that, regardless of origin, rhythm is a universal path back to the self. - Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? I've just arrived back in Sydney for a few months to spend time with my family and play some shows. I've been putting the finishing touches on some new records and have started to put a live set together for 2026. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? The mix was recorded in my parents' basement, where I live when I'm back in Australia. I've played a few closing sets recently, so I was channeling that energy a little. There are some moments that I extracted from last year that were particularly satisfying to me as well as some WIPs/demos from myself and producers I admire. I feel that there is a lot crammed into these 90 minutes (in a good way, I hope). What are you looking forward to in the near future? I've got some fun shows to play during my Australia trip. I'm looking forward to releasing various new and exciting projects with friends and going to the beach. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? The ethnic cleansing of Palestine by !sra*l and its allies. I'm mentioning it here even though it doesn't feel right to call it a "political" cause, because it implies two equally valid sides to debate. It suggests you can simply "stay out of politics" and remain willfully ignorant of a genocide. Living in Germany, you encounter this rhetoric everywhere. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? Growing up in Sydney there was a club that we used to frequent called Goodgod Smallclub (RIP) in Chinatown. I feel like that was the closest thing we had at the time to what people in Europe and the USA experience in terms of having a regular nightclub they can go to and get into dance music. Anyway, Cliques (RIP?) used to have an occasional night at Goodgod where they'd play all night long. It was a lot of UK techno and dubstep, which had an obvious effect on me.
translation.blurb: A kaleidoscope of polyrhythms and post-dubstep.
